cases.prompt: write documentation for my python package
set up a docs folder with tutorials and how-to guides for this repo
our docs are a mess, restructure them so people can find things
apply diataxis to our documentation
can u write a getting started guide and usage docs for this lib
draft a documentation plan for the v1 release
docs r just one giant markdown file, split it up properly
the tutorial keeps drifting into api reference material, clean that up
what pages should a small OSS package's documentation actually have?
write a how-to guide for configuring webhooks in our tool
review our documentation and tell me what's missing
we renamed half the config keys in v2, bring the docs up to date
write a README for this project
my readme is bloated, restructure it
deploy our docs to github pages
set up mkdocs with the material theme for this repo
the docs site build is failing in CI
write CONTRIBUTING.md and a code of conduct
add docstrings to the functions in utils.py
add topics and a social preview image so the repo gets found
generate a changelog from the commit history
Our Python library has a 700-line README and no docs. Create a proper documentation set.
expected_behavior: inventories existing content first and maps each chunk to tutorial / how-to / reference / explanation before writing anything new
proposes a minimal viable doc set (getting started, a few task how-tos, reference, 1-2 concept pages plus FAQ/troubleshooting) instead of a sprawling 20-page tree
moves README depth into docs/ pages and leaves the README as pitch + quickstart + links (or routes README rewriting to readme-author) rather than duplicating content in both places
keeps modes separate - the tutorial has no theory digressions, reference stays dry and factual
every command or code snippet is real and runnable in this repo, never invented

cases.prompt: Write a getting-started tutorial for our CLI tool
expected_behavior: writes a learning-oriented, on-rails lesson: one happy path, concrete numbered steps, no configuration detours or design discussion
starts from a stated clean starting point (install + prerequisites) and ends with a visible, checkable success
uses second person, imperative, present tense, with short sentences
verifies the commands against the actual repo (or clearly marks anything unverifiable) instead of inventing flags

cases.prompt: Apply diataxis to our docs but we only have capacity to maintain about 4 pages
expected_behavior: scales the framework down instead of refusing or fabricating a large tree: roughly one quickstart tutorial, one how-to collection page, one reference page, one concepts page
explains what was deliberately dropped and what signal (user questions, issues) should trigger adding it back
does not mix modes within the 4 pages even though the set is small

cases.prompt: We shipped v2 last week - renamed the config keys and removed the legacy sync command. Update the docs.
expected_behavior: diffs or inspects the actual code changes first, then searches every docs page for the old identifiers rather than only editing the obvious page
updates or prunes each hit; deprecated-but-working features get marked with their replacement instead of silently deleted
preserves the existing docs structure and tone - a sync, not a rewrite
reports anything it could not verify against the code
